rationale: This is a canonical example of the Houben-Hoesch reaction, featuring the acylation of a highly activated, electron-rich aromatic ring (1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene) with a simple nitrile (acetonitrile). The reaction is catalyzed by a Lewis acid (ZnCl2) and HCl, followed by aqueous workup to hydrolyze the intermediate ketimine, yielding the final aryl ketone product. This example clearly illustrates the key transformation: the formation of a C(aryl)-C(acyl) bond from an arene and a nitrile.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses a simple, highly activated arene (1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene) and a simple nitrile (acetonitrile). It avoids using phenol directly as the substrate, which could be confused with Fries or Kolbe-Schmitt reactions. It also avoids complex structures or other functional groups that might trigger unrelated named reaction classifiers. The core transformation is specific to the nitrile-based acylation of an activated arene, characteristic of the Houben-Hoesch reaction.
